tasks.title: Assess ratatui-ecosystem crates for TUI enhancement opportunities
evidence: User request: 'Create a task or series of tasks to have the project integrate with well-known ratatui crates that could enhance our TUI'
User goal: Improve user experience, reduce development responsibilities to maintain custom code
User constraint: Do not integrate crates that are no longer maintained
Candidate crates: tui-widgets, ratatui-explorer, ratatui-image, ratatui-textarea
Related task RQ-0406: OpenTUI assessment - this assessment should consider compatibility with that decision
Related task RQ-0398: TUI resize handling issues indicate current TUI has maintenance burden
Related task RQ-0405: Color support improvements planned - ecosystem crates may provide better theming
plan: Research each candidate crate on crates.io and docs.rs for maintenance status and activity
Check GitHub repositories for commit activity, open issues, and maintenance indicators
Inventory current custom TUI code that could be replaced by ecosystem crates
Assess tui-widgets: additional widgets that could replace custom implementations
Assess ratatui-explorer: file explorer widget for potential file browsing features
Assess ratatui-image: image support for potential logo/screenshot display
Assess ratatui-textarea: better text input for task editing, search, or configuration
Document maintenance status: last commit, release cadence, issue response time
Document integration effort: API compatibility, breaking changes, dependency overhead
Document benefits: reduced LOC, improved UX, community-tested code
Provide prioritized recommendations: which crates to adopt, in what order
Generate assessment report at /tmp/ratatui-ecosystem-assessment.md
notes: Assessment is research-only; no code changes required
Consider overlap with OpenTUI assessment (RQ-0406) - if OpenTUI is adopted, some ecosystem crates may be redundant
Focus on crates that solve real pain points in current TUI implementation
ratatui-textarea may be most immediately useful for text input improvements
tasks.request: Create a task or series of tasks to have the project integrate with well-known ralph crates that could enhance our TUI and eliminate some of our custom code overhead. This will improve user experience, reduce development responsibilities to maintain lots of custom code, and overall improve the TUI part of the app. Below is a starter list of crates to assess. Do Not integrate crates that are no longer maintained. Adopt ratatui Ecosystem Crates: tui-widgets, ratatui-explorer, ratatui-image, ratatui-textarea

tasks.title: Implement migration system for config and README changes
evidence: User request: 'We need to consider migrations when we implement changes to the config, .ralph/README.md, etc.'
User concern: Users may modify .ralph/README.md but we need to update it when Ralph changes
User concern: Config key renames should migrate user values automatically
User concern: Default file changes (e.g., queue.json to queue.jsonc) need safe migration
User context: Rapid development means frequent changes that need seamless user experience
plan: Design migration tracking system (version metadata for .ralph/ files)
Add RALPH_README_VERSION header detection and migration logic for .ralph/README.md
Implement config key migration framework with rename mappings
Add queue.json to queue.jsonc migration path with safe fallback
Create 'ralph migrate' command to check and apply pending migrations
Integrate automatic migration checks into 'ralph init' and startup
Add migration history tracking to prevent duplicate migrations
Document migration patterns for future developers
Run 'make ci' to validate changes
tasks.request: We need to consider migrations when we implement changes to the config, .ralph/README.md, etc. Users aren't updating .ralph/READEME.md, or at least shouldn't be as it is mainly there for agents, but they might, but when we change content of the READEME.md we need to ensure we are updating .ralph/READEME.md in other projects after they run ralph after updating. Same thing for checking config. If we change the name of a config key to a new value we should be migrating that value for the user and keeping what they set that config param to. If we decide to change the default task queue.json to queue.jsonc (which we just added support for) then we should consider migrating that safely too. These are things that users expect to be seamless.

tasks.title: Verify and document reasoning_effort field support in task queue
evidence: User request: 'verify we have support for model_effort in the task queue'
User context: reasoning_effort is primarily beneficial for Codex runner with GPT models
User context: Acceptable values are low, medium, high, xhigh (xhigh should be used rarely)
User context: Purpose is to dynamically override model reasoning effort without changing config
User context: Other models may not support reasoning effort like Codex does
plan: Verify reasoning_effort field exists in Task struct and is optional
Verify task builder supports --effort flag with low/medium/high/xhigh values
Verify scan command supports --effort flag
Verify effort value is passed through to Codex runner correctly
Verify unsupported runners gracefully ignore the field
Add/update documentation in docs/cli.md for --effort flag
Add/update documentation in docs/queue-and-tasks.md for reasoning_effort field
Add warning when xhigh is used (consumes limits extremely fast)
Run 'make ci' to validate changes
tasks.request: I believe we added support for model_effort in the task queue. this is primarily only beneficial for the codex runner with GPT models but just wanted to make sure we have support for that and it is documented. If it is added during task creation and an unsupported runner/model is used it is just ignored (intended) but it doesn't hurt to have it added. Acceptable values are (low, medium, high, xhigh). xhigh should only be used very rarely as it consumes usage limits extremely fast. both task builder and scan can utilize this field as long as we clarify its purpose. the purpose is to dynamically override the model reasoning effort via this optional field so users can run loops without having to stop and change their config as varying levels of tasks are in progress.

tasks.title: Design and implement local release workflow without GitHub Actions
evidence: User request: 'We require proper versioning and changelog generation'
User request: 'Figure out a way to properly bump versions'
User concern: Project synced with private git repo, hesitant to go public
User concern: Missing mechanisms to build and publish releases to GitHub with proper version control, changelogs
User constraint: No GitHub Actions are enabled or allowed on this project
Current state: Makefile has 'make release VERSION=x.y.z' target but may need enhancement
Current state: CHANGELOG.md exists with Keep a Changelog format
Current state: Version is 0.1.0 in crates/ralph/Cargo.toml
plan: Audit current 'make release' target and identify gaps for local-only release workflow
Design local release script that: validates version, updates Cargo.toml, updates CHANGELOG, creates git tag, builds release binary
Add GitHub CLI (gh) integration for creating releases locally without Actions
Ensure release artifacts (binary, checksums) are generated and attached to GitHub release
Add pre-release validation: ensure 'make ci' passes, ensure working directory is clean, ensure on main branch
Add support for generating release notes from CHANGELOG section
Create docs/releasing.md documenting the complete local release workflow
Test the workflow with a patch version bump (0.1.1) to verify end-to-end
Update README.md with release/versioning information if needed
tasks.request: We require proper versioning and changelog generation. Figure out a way to properly bump versions. Project is still synced with a private git repo. user has hesitancy to go public due to missing mechanisms to build and publish releases to his github with proper version control, changelogs, etc. no github actions are enabled or allowed on this project.

plan: Verify 'ralph --version' works via clap derive
Add 'ralph version' subcommand that prints version, build info, and git commit hash
Consider adding version to TUI header if not already present
Ensure version is embedded at compile time via CARGO_PKG_VERSION
Run 'make ci' to validate changes

Ensure generated changelog follows Keep a Changelog format

tasks.title: Add color support to CLI and TUI for enhanced user experience
evidence: User request: 'we should be using colors in the CLI and TUI whenever possible'
User context: Colors should guide users to important information during long 'ralph run loop' tasks
User context: Current output can be bland white text in black box
User suggestion: Color-code agent reasoning/thinking blocks, tool calls, supervisor calls
User constraint: Don't colorize everything like a preschool art class - find balance
plan: Audit current CLI output for opportunities to add semantic coloring
Audit TUI rendering for color enhancement opportunities
Design color palette: success (green), error (red), warning (yellow), info (blue), emphasis (cyan/magenta)
Add color support for agent reasoning/thinking blocks (subtle gray/blue)
Add color support for tool calls (distinct color like cyan)
Add color support for supervisor/system messages (distinct color like magenta)
Add color support for task status indicators (todo/doing/done)
Add --no-color flag and NO_COLOR env var support for accessibility
Ensure colors work well on both light and dark terminal backgrounds
Run 'make ci' to validate changes
tasks.request: we should be using colors in the CLI and TUI whenever possible to enhance the user experience. Users should be guided by colors, especially but not limited to when long 'ralph run loop' tasks are ongoing, so they can focus on the important information and not get clouded by verbosity. I think the verbosity is good right now but can sometimes be bland when a user runs 'ralph run loop' and sees nothing but white text inside a black box. Maybe we look to color code agent reasoning/thinking blocks, tool calls, supervisor calls, etc? We dont have to colorize everything to make it look like a preschool art class but a nice balance might be beneficial for user expereince.

tasks.title: Unify CLI argument patterns: make 'ralph scan' accept positional prompt like 'ralph task'
evidence: User reports: 'ralph task' works by appending prompt without a flag, but 'ralph scan' requires '--focus' flag
This creates inconsistent UX between two similar commands
Both commands support '--runner' and '--effort' flags, so the prompt syntax should align
plan: Review current 'ralph task' argument parsing to understand how it accepts positional prompt
Review current 'ralph scan' argument parsing and '--focus' flag implementation
Modify 'ralph scan' to accept an optional positional prompt argument (in addition to --focus for backward compatibility)
Update help text and examples for 'ralph scan' to show both usage patterns
Update docs/cli.md to reflect the unified syntax
Run 'make ci' to validate changes
tasks.request: 'ralph task' works by just appending the prompt to the 'ralph task' command without a flag. 'ralph scan' requires the prompt to follow after '--focus'. this seems like an inconsistent user experience. i do use both with different '--runner' and '--effort' flags but it seems odd to have to specify '--focus' for the 'scan' command.

tasks.title: Add scan mode for feature discovery/innovation vs code hygiene
evidence: User reports: 'ralph scan' defaults to looking for repo rules violations, inconsistent/incomplete code (break-fix maintenance)
User wants ability to scan for new features to add, feature completeness for specific use-cases
Current behavior stays focused on code hygiene even when directed toward innovation
Need to balance without overcorrecting away from the useful maintenance mode
plan: Review current scan prompt template to understand how it guides the model
Review existing scan flags to see if there's already a mode toggle
Design a '--mode' or similar flag with values like 'maintenance' (default) and 'innovation'
Create separate prompt paths or conditional prompt sections for each mode
Ensure 'maintenance' mode preserves current break-fix behavior
Ensure 'innovation' mode guides model toward feature gaps, use-case completeness, and enhancement opportunities
Update CLI help text with examples for both modes
Update docs/cli.md with mode descriptions
Run 'make ci' to validate changes
tasks.request: right now when 'ralph scan' is run i think it defaults to looking for repo rules violations, inconsistent or incomplete code, etc. More of a break-fix maintenance order. This works well. But it would be nice to be able to have it look for new features to add. maybe the prompt already has this verbiage for innovation in it, or maybe there is already a flag for it that i have forgotten about, but even when i use the '--focus' and direct the model to look for feature completeness, adding new features for a specific use-case or in general, it seems to stay focused on the code hygiene as aspect much more. Not sure how to handle this without overcorrecting.

tasks.title: Fix TUI terminal resize handling and visual glitches
evidence: User reports: TUI formatting breaks on terminal resize until next update
In some cases formatting remains broken even after updates
Logs need to stream and display properly
User has 'GREAT success with the CLI and barely touch the TUI due to poor user experience'
This is a polish issue that affects user adoption
plan: Review current TUI event handling to understand resize signal processing
Review current rendering logic to identify why redraw doesn't happen immediately on resize
Implement proper terminal resize event handling that triggers immediate full redraw
Ensure layout calculations use current terminal dimensions after resize
Test log streaming display during and after resize operations
Review and fix any remaining visual glitches (flickering, misalignment, truncation)
Test across common terminal emulators (iTerm2, Terminal.app, Alacritty, etc.)
Run 'make ci' to validate changes
tasks.request: we must review the TUI resizing to ensure that when a user resizes their terminal that the screen is redrawn. currently i see a whole lot of formatting break and look like crap until the next update is sent to the screen, and in some cases it still is busted. this is a tough problem to solve, tuis are hard. but we must make sure we have a tui that is performant and without visual glitches. logs need to stream and be displayed properly. user flows and experience need to be polished otherwise it looks like amateur work and users wont want to use it. personally, i have had GREAT success with the CLI and barely touch the TUI due to poor user experience. The CLI version of the 'ralph task', 'ralph scan', and 'ralph run' commands have been excellent.
